elderScrollsViReleaseDate.title: The Elder Scrolls VI Release Date
elderScrollsViReleaseDate.subtitle: Bethesda has not announced a release date. Follow the game's progression from the 2018 reveal to early playable builds.
elderScrollsViReleaseDate.intro: Bethesda announced The Elder Scrolls VI after Fallout 76 at E3 2018. Development remained in planning and pre-production while Bethesda Game Studios focused on Starfield, before moving into active production and internal early builds.

items.title: The Elder Scrolls VI Is Revealed
items.description: Bethesda Game Studios unveiled a short landscape teaser during its E3 2018 showcase. The announcement confirmed the project but did not reveal its setting, platforms, gameplay, or release date.

items.title: Planning and Pre-Production
items.description: The project remained behind Starfield in Bethesda Game Studios' development schedule. Early work focused on technology, design concepts, world planning, and the systems needed for the next main Elder Scrolls game.

items.title: Early Development Begins
items.description: Following the completion and launch of Starfield, Bethesda described The Elder Scrolls VI as being in early development. The studio began shifting more development resources toward the project.

items.title: Early Builds Are Being Played
items.description: Bethesda confirmed that developers were already playing early versions of the game. This marked a transition from planning work toward playable environments, mechanics, and internal prototypes.

items.title: Microsoft Court Document Window
items.description: Documents published during Microsoft's legal proceedings listed the game as expected in 2026 or later. This was an internal planning range rather than a public Bethesda launch announcement.

items.title: No Release Date Announced
items.description: Bethesda has not announced a release year, launch day, preorder schedule, or public gameplay showcase. The game remains an active long-term project at Bethesda Game Studios.

elderScrollsViPlatforms.title: The Elder Scrolls VI Platforms
elderScrollsViPlatforms.subtitle: Xbox and PC have appeared in Microsoft's internal planning documents, while Bethesda has not published a final platform list.
elderScrollsViPlatforms.intro: The Elder Scrolls VI is being developed under Xbox-owned Bethesda Game Studios. Its long development cycle means final hardware targets may depend on the console generation available near release.

items.details: Microsoft court documents associated the game with Xbox and PC. Bethesda has not released a final store page or console-specific technical specification.
Mainline Bethesda Game Studios titles traditionally launch on Windows PC, and Microsoft documentation listed PC alongside Xbox for The Elder Scrolls VI.
The game does not yet have a public Game Pass page, release date, subscription tier, or preload information.
Bethesda and Xbox have not published a PlayStation 5 version, release schedule, store listing, or exclusivity statement for the finished game.
The game's long production schedule could place it near a newer Xbox hardware generation, but Bethesda has not named any next-generation console.
A Steam release has not been formally listed. Bethesda's recent major PC releases have supported both the Microsoft Store and Steam.
No Nintendo version, cloud edition, port, or technical target has been announced.
Bethesda has not confirmed Xbox Cloud Gaming availability, streaming performance targets, or supported devices.

elderScrollsViTrailer.title: The Elder Scrolls VI Trailer
elderScrollsViTrailer.subtitle: The official 2018 announcement teaser remains the primary public video. It reveals a large coastal landscape but contains no characters, combat, interface, or gameplay systems.
elderScrollsViTrailer.intro: Bethesda introduced The Elder Scrolls VI with a brief cinematic flyover at its E3 2018 showcase. The footage established the project's scale and fantasy atmosphere while keeping its province, story, era, and central conflict hidden.

featuredVideo.title: The Elder Scrolls VI – Official Announcement Teaser

items.title: Official Announcement Teaser
details: An aerial camera moves across a mountainous coastal landscape.
The environment includes cliffs, valleys, water, roads, and distant settlements.
The Elder Scrolls theme accompanies the landscape reveal.
The final title card confirms The Elder Scrolls VI.
No playable character, combat system, interface, dialogue, or quest footage appears.

items.title: Gameplay Reveal
details: No official gameplay demonstration has been published.
Character creation, exploration, combat, magic, dialogue, and quest systems have not been shown.
Bethesda has not announced a date for the first gameplay presentation.

items.title: Developer Deep Dive
details: No dedicated development documentary has been released.
Bethesda has not publicly demonstrated the game's world-building tools or Creation Engine technology.
Future developer videos may cover the setting, factions, exploration systems, and technical design.

items.title: Launch Trailer
details: No launch trailer or release campaign is available.
Preorders, editions, bonuses, and launch platforms have not been presented in video form.
This entry will follow the official release marketing schedule.

elderScrollsViSettingAndMap.title: The Elder Scrolls VI Setting and Map
elderScrollsViSettingAndMap.subtitle: Bethesda has not named the game's province, but the announcement teaser has produced strong Hammerfell, High Rock, and Iliac Bay theories.
elderScrollsViSettingAndMap.intro: The Elder Scrolls VI will return to Tamriel, the continent used by the main numbered Elder Scrolls games. Its exact province remains unrevealed, leaving the teaser landscape as the main source for comparing possible locations.

evidence: The teaser shows a large mountainous landmass beside a substantial body of water.
Cliffs, valleys, paths, ruins, and distant settlements are visible.
The landscape contains both dry rocky areas and greener elevated terrain.
No readable map, province name, city name, flag, or faction symbol appears.
items.summary: The footage confirms a large coastal fantasy region but does not identify its province.

evidence: Hammerfell contains dry mountains, deserts, rocky coastlines, and major port settlements.
Its western and northern landscapes can resemble the rugged terrain shown in the teaser.
The province is the homeland of the Redguards.
Important locations include Sentinel, Hegathe, Taneth, Rihad, Dragonstar, and the Alik'r Desert.
items.summary: Hammerfell is the most prominent single-province theory connected to the teaser's dry coastal geography.

evidence: High Rock is known for steep mountains, green valleys, castles, and fragmented coastal kingdoms.
The province is the homeland of the Bretons.
Its major regions include Daggerfall, Wayrest, Evermore, Northpoint, and the Wrothgarian Mountains.
The teaser's cliffs and distant fortified locations match parts of High Rock's established geography.
items.summary: High Rock remains a major theory because of the teaser's mountainous coastline and medieval-looking settlements.

evidence: The Iliac Bay separates southern High Rock from northern Hammerfell.
A regional map could include Breton kingdoms, Redguard cities, islands, ports, mountains, and desert areas.
The region previously served as the central setting of The Elder Scrolls II: Daggerfall.
A two-province structure would support naval travel and strong political conflict between neighboring cultures.
items.summary: The Iliac Bay theory combines the strongest Hammerfell and High Rock interpretations into one connected game world.

evidence: Bethesda's newer technology supports larger environments and denser exploration spaces.
A border-spanning story could connect Hammerfell, High Rock, Orsinium, and nearby islands.
The announcement teaser does not establish the final boundaries of the playable map.
items.summary: A multi-region map could extend beyond one province, especially around the Iliac Bay and western Tamriel.

evidence: No official trailer or statement presents every Tamriel province as playable.
The teaser displays only one continuous landscape.
No complete continental map, travel system, or province list has been shown.
items.summary: The entire continent has not been announced as the playable map for The Elder Scrolls VI.

elderScrollsViGameplay.title: The Elder Scrolls VI Gameplay
elderScrollsViGameplay.subtitle: Explore the confirmed direction of Bethesda's next single-player fantasy RPG and the core systems inherited from earlier Elder Scrolls games.
elderScrollsViGameplay.intro: The Elder Scrolls VI returns to Tamriel as Bethesda Game Studios' next major open-world role-playing game. Detailed mechanics have not been publicly demonstrated, but exploration, character freedom, quests, combat, magic, and environmental storytelling remain central to the series.

items.title: A New Open World in Tamriel
items.description: Players will explore another large region of Tamriel filled with wilderness, settlements, ruins, dungeons, and environmental stories. Bethesda has described the game as the next chapter in The Elder Scrolls series.

items.title: First-Person and Third-Person Combat
items.description: The series traditionally supports melee weapons, bows, shields, stealth attacks, and offensive magic from first-person or third-person viewpoints. Skyrim's blocking, dual-wielding, and equipment-based combat provide the clearest foundation for the next game.

items.title: Spells, Enchanting, and Magical Builds
items.description: Destruction, Restoration, Conjuration, Illusion, and Alteration are the main modern spell schools. Enchanting, magical artifacts, racial abilities, and specialized mage builds are established parts of Elder Scrolls character progression.

items.title: Main Story, Factions, and Side Adventures
items.description: The game is being designed as a full single-player role-playing experience. The Elder Scrolls structure normally combines a central conflict with faction questlines, town stories, Daedric quests, dungeon objectives, and smaller encounters discovered while exploring.

items.title: Build a Character Through Skills
items.description: Previous games improve skills through use and connect those skills to levels, attributes, or perks. Players can traditionally combine warrior, mage, thief, assassin, archer, alchemist, and hybrid playstyles without selecting a permanently locked class.

items.title: Conversations, Persuasion, and Player Decisions
items.description: Dialogue is expected to support quest discovery, persuasion, intimidation, bribery, and faction interactions. Earlier games also allow players to choose quest outcomes, support competing groups, and change how individual characters respond.

items.title: Cities, Villages, Homes, and Local Economies
items.description: Tamriel's settlements traditionally contain shops, inns, guild halls, temples, homes, guards, and local quest networks. Property ownership, crafting stations, merchants, crime systems, and regional politics are established open-world features.

items.title: Dynamic Encounters and Environmental Interaction
items.description: Bethesda worlds combine scheduled NPC behavior, wildlife, physics objects, random encounters, crime responses, and unscripted conflicts. These systems allow quests and exploration events to overlap instead of functioning as isolated activities.

elderScrollsViDevelopmentTimeline.title: The Elder Scrolls VI Development Timeline
elderScrollsViDevelopmentTimeline.subtitle: Follow the game's progress from its 2018 announcement through pre-production, early builds, and active development.
elderScrollsViDevelopmentTimeline.intro: Bethesda announced The Elder Scrolls VI years before full production because Starfield was the studio's immediate priority. Development moved forward through engine preparation, pre-production, and playable internal builds before becoming Bethesda Game Studios' next major project.

items.title: The Elder Scrolls VI Is Revealed
items.description: Bethesda announced the game during its E3 2018 showcase with a short landscape teaser. The reveal confirmed that a sixth main Elder Scrolls game was planned after Starfield.

items.title: Concept and Technology Planning
items.description: The project remained in an early planning stage while Bethesda concentrated its main production resources on Starfield. World concepts, technology requirements, and the game's long-term creative direction were developed during this period.

items.title: Creation Engine 2 Becomes the Technical Baseline
items.description: Starfield became Bethesda's first game built on Creation Engine 2. Its renderer, animation pipeline, procedural tools, and updated development systems created the technical foundation available to the Elder Scrolls VI team.

items.title: Development Expands After Starfield
items.description: As Starfield approached release, Bethesda began shifting more developers and production attention toward The Elder Scrolls VI. The project moved beyond its long pre-production period into early development.

items.title: Bethesda Mentions Early Internal Builds
items.description: In a studio message celebrating The Elder Scrolls anniversary, Bethesda said developers were returning to Tamriel and playing early builds of the new game.

items.title: World and Gameplay Systems Take Shape
items.description: Production focused on building the playable world, core role-playing systems, quests, technology, and content pipelines required for Bethesda's next large single-player release.

items.title: Bethesda Discusses Continued Progress
items.description: Bethesda provided another development update as work continued on the game. The project remained positioned as the studio's next major fantasy role-playing game after Starfield.

items.title: Bethesda Builds the Next Version of Tamriel
items.description: The development team is working across world design, gameplay, quests, art, animation, audio, and engine technology. No public gameplay demonstration or final launch date has been released.

elderScrollsViCreationEngine3.title: The Elder Scrolls VI Creation Engine 3
elderScrollsViCreationEngine3.subtitle: Compare Bethesda's older engine systems, Starfield's Creation Engine 2 upgrades, and the technology requirements of the next Elder Scrolls world.
elderScrollsViCreationEngine3.intro: The Elder Scrolls VI is being developed after Bethesda's major Creation Engine overhaul for Starfield. Bethesda has not published a formal Creation Engine 3 specification, but the next engine iteration can build on Creation Engine 2 while focusing on denser landscapes, cities, characters, combat, and fantasy-world simulation.

items.earlierGames: Oblivion used Gamebryo technology, while Skyrim and Fallout 4 used generations of Bethesda's original Creation Engine.
items.starfieldBaseline: Starfield introduced Creation Engine 2 with major changes to rendering, animation, tools, and large-scale world production.
items.tesViDirection: A further-developed engine branch designed around a dense fantasy province, handcrafted quests, and long-term content support.

items.earlierGames: Older games used region cells, limited distant detail, and simpler material, shadow, and lighting systems.
items.starfieldBaseline: Creation Engine 2 added improved materials, lighting, volumetric effects, terrain rendering, and more detailed environments.
items.tesViDirection: Large natural landscapes, detailed cities, stronger weather effects, improved water, longer sightlines, and richer vegetation.

items.earlierGames: Cities, buildings, caves, and large regions frequently used separate loading transitions.
items.starfieldBaseline: Faster storage and updated streaming support larger environments, although many ships, interiors, and locations remain separated.
items.tesViDirection: Faster regional streaming and smoother movement between wilderness, settlements, interiors, and underground spaces.

items.earlierGames: Skyrim used a legacy animation system for movement, melee attacks, conversations, and creature behavior.
items.starfieldBaseline: Starfield expanded facial animation, locomotion, weapon handling, and character interaction systems.
items.tesViDirection: More responsive melee combat, improved spell casting, better creature movement, and more expressive dialogue scenes.

items.earlierGames: Radiant AI gave NPCs schedules, homes, jobs, and basic reactions to crimes or nearby events.
items.starfieldBaseline: NPCs support combat roles, crew assignments, settlement activity, and large numbers of quest interactions.
items.tesViDirection: Deeper daily routines, faction relationships, dynamic encounters, coordinated combat, and stronger reactions to player reputation.

items.earlierGames: Loose objects, traps, bodies, weapons, and environmental items could react through physics simulation.
items.starfieldBaseline: The engine supports large quantities of movable objects, ship interiors, low-gravity movement, and zero-gravity combat.
items.tesViDirection: Improved environmental interaction for weapons, spells, traps, doors, clutter, destructible objects, and dungeon mechanisms.

items.earlierGames: Bethesda assembled provinces from handcrafted exterior cells, dungeons, towns, and scripted quest locations.
items.starfieldBaseline: Procedural terrain and location-placement tools support a very large universe alongside handcrafted cities and quests.
items.tesViDirection: Procedural tools can establish terrain and natural detail while developers concentrate handcrafted work on settlements, dungeons, and story locations.

items.earlierGames: The Creation Kit and plugin system enabled new quests, locations, items, characters, and total conversions.
items.starfieldBaseline: Creation Kit 2 supports official mod creation and Bethesda's modern content pipeline.
items.tesViDirection: A data-driven world structure suited to long-term community mods, custom quests, new regions, equipment, and gameplay overhauls.

elderScrollsViStoryAndLore.title: The Elder Scrolls VI Story and Lore
elderScrollsViStoryAndLore.subtitle: Review the established history of Tamriel, major regional conflicts, and the lore connected to the game's possible setting.
elderScrollsViStoryAndLore.intro: Bethesda has confirmed that The Elder Scrolls VI will return players to Tamriel, but it has not published the protagonist, central conflict, or exact historical date. Hammerfell and High Rock remain the main setting theories because of the reveal trailer's landscape and the political storylines established around the Iliac Bay region.

items.summary: The game is the sixth main entry in The Elder Scrolls series and takes place somewhere in Tamriel.
details: Bethesda revealed a mountainous coastal landscape in the announcement teaser.
The playable character, main antagonist, and central quest have not been introduced.
The exact province and historical year have not been published.
The story follows the events of the earlier main Elder Scrolls games within the same shared universe.

items.summary: Hammerfell is the homeland of the Redguards and contains deserts, mountains, ports, ancient ruins, and politically divided kingdoms.
details: Major locations include Sentinel, Hegathe, Dragonstar, Rihad, Taneth, Elinhir, and the Alik'r Desert.
Redguard politics have historically been divided between the traditionalist Crowns and the more cosmopolitan Forebears.
Hammerfell fought the Aldmeri Dominion after the Empire accepted the White-Gold Concordat.
The Second Treaty of Stros M'Kai ended the conflict in 4E 180 and required Dominion forces to withdraw from Hammerfell.

items.section: High Rock and the Iliac Bay
items.summary: High Rock is the homeland of the Bretons and is known for feudal kingdoms, noble rivalries, knightly orders, and powerful magical traditions.
details: Important cities include Daggerfall, Wayrest, Camlorn, and Northpoint.
High Rock and western Hammerfell surround the Iliac Bay, the setting of The Elder Scrolls II: Daggerfall.
Breton society combines human, elven, and Direnni cultural influences.
The region's fragmented kingdoms provide a natural setting for diplomacy, succession disputes, and faction warfare.

items.section: The Aldmeri Dominion and the Empire
items.summary: The Fourth Era is shaped by the weakened Empire, the rise of the Aldmeri Dominion, and the unresolved consequences of the Great War.
details: The White-Gold Concordat ended the Great War between the Empire and the Dominion.
The treaty outlawed Talos worship and transferred disputed territory in southern Hammerfell.
Hammerfell rejected the Imperial settlement and continued fighting independently.
The political balance between the Empire, Dominion, and independent provinces remains one of Tamriel's largest unresolved conflicts.

items.summary: Modern Elder Scrolls games traditionally allow players to create characters from ten major Tamrielic races.
details: Human races: Breton, Imperial, Nord, and Redguard.
Elven races: Altmer, Bosmer, Dunmer, and Orsimer.
Beast races: Argonian and Khajiit.
Each race traditionally receives different starting bonuses, resistances, powers, or cultural dialogue.

items.summary: A Hammerfell or Iliac Bay game can draw from Redguard houses, Breton kingdoms, Orc strongholds, and international political forces.
details: Crown and Forebear political groups represent competing Redguard traditions.
Breton noble houses and knightly orders control separate kingdoms and territories.
Orsinium and the Orc clans have a long history of conflict with neighboring Breton and Redguard powers.
Imperial agents, Thalmor operatives, merchants, pirates, mercenaries, and Daedric cults can connect regional stories to wider Tamriel.

items.summary: The Redguards descend from Yokuda, a lost western continent whose warriors, gods, and magical traditions still shape Hammerfell.
details: The Ra Gada migrations established Redguard rule across much of Hammerfell.
Sword-singers were warriors who could manifest spirit weapons known as Shehai.
Yokudan religion includes deities such as Ruptga, Tu'whacca, Zeht, Tava, and the HoonDing.
Ancient Yokudan ruins and lost sword techniques provide major foundations for regional quests and artifacts.

items.summary: The strongest setting-based themes connect Hammerfell's independence, Dominion pressure, and the unstable politics of the Iliac Bay.
details: A renewed conflict between Hammerfell and the Aldmeri Dominion.
Competition between Crown and Forebear leaders over Hammerfell's future.
Breton succession disputes and alliances between Iliac Bay kingdoms.
The restoration or destruction of Orsinium.
The return of sword-singer traditions or another ancient Yokudan power.

faq.title: Frequently Asked
faq.subtitle: Everything we know so far about The Elder Scrolls VI

questions.question: When is The Elder Scrolls VI release date?
questions.answer: Bethesda has not announced a release date for The Elder Scrolls VI. The game remains in active development as Bethesda Game Studios' next major project, with no public launch window, preorder schedule, or gameplay showcase confirmed.

questions.question: What platforms will The Elder Scrolls VI be on?
questions.answer: The Elder Scrolls VI is being developed by Xbox-owned Bethesda Game Studios. Xbox and PC have appeared in Microsoft's internal planning documents, but Bethesda has not published a final platform list, and PlayStation, Game Pass, and next-generation support remain without dedicated announcements.

questions.question: Where is The Elder Scrolls VI set?
questions.answer: Bethesda has not named the province. The Elder Scrolls VI announcement teaser has produced strong Hammerfell, High Rock, and Iliac Bay setting theories based on its mountainous coastal landscape, but no official location has been confirmed.

questions.question: Is The Elder Scrolls VI an Xbox exclusive?
questions.answer: The Elder Scrolls VI has not been confirmed as an Xbox exclusive. Older Microsoft court documents listed an Xbox and PC release window, but Bethesda and Xbox have not published a final exclusivity statement for the finished game.

questions.question: What engine does The Elder Scrolls VI use?
questions.answer: The Elder Scrolls VI is being built on Bethesda's next Creation Engine iteration, building on the Creation Engine 2 technology introduced with Starfield. Bethesda has not published a formal Creation Engine 3 specification yet.

questions.question: Where can I watch The Elder Scrolls VI trailer?
questions.answer: The official The Elder Scrolls VI announcement teaser from E3 2018 is the primary public video, available on Bethesda's YouTube channel. No gameplay reveal, developer deep dive, or launch trailer has been released so far.
